sourceDetail: UWorld QID 52617
stem: The nurse is assessing a client with suspected acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). Which of the following findings would support a diagnosis of ARDS? Select all that apply.
options: anxiety
tachypnea
bradycardia
blood-tinged sputum
intercostal retractions
rationale:
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) is a life-threatening respiratory condition that occurs when injury to lung tissue damages the alveolar-capillary membrane.  This damage allows fluid and proteins to cross the alveolar-capillary membrane and fill the alveoli, causing profound hypoxia and organ damage.  Clinical manifestations of ARDS include:

Refractory hypoxemia (ie, decreased capillary oxygen saturation despite administration of high concentrations of oxygen) because supplemental oxygen cannot cross the alveolar-capillary membrane

Mental status changes (eg, anxiety, confusion) secondary to impaired perfusion of vital organs (eg, brain) (Option 1)

Tachypnea (ie, increased respiratory rate), dyspnea (ie, shortness of breath), and increased work of breathing (ie, accessory muscle use, intercostal retractions) as compensatory responses to hypoxia (Options 2 and 5)

Crackles due to pulmonary edema (ie, fluid buildup in the alveoli)

(Option 3)  Clients with ARDS experience tachycardia (ie, elevated heart rate), not bradycardia (ie, decreased heart rate), as a compensatory response to hypotension and widespread hypoxia.

(Option 4)  Bloody sputum may occur in upper respiratory infections and lung cancer but is not a manifestation of ARDS.

Educational objective: Acute respiratory distress syndrome is a life-threatening respiratory condition that occurs when injury to lung tissue damages the alveolar-capillary membrane. Manifestations include refractory hypoxemia, anxiety, tachypnea, intercostal retractions, increased work of breathing, and crackles.

sourceDetail: UWorld QID 52678
stem: The nurse is assessing a client with suspected anaphylaxis. Which of the following findings would support a diagnosis of anaphylaxis? Select all that apply.
options: urticaria
wheezing
swollen lips
S3 heart tone
unequal pupils
rationale:
Anaphylaxis is an immune-mediated, life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction to an allergen (eg, insect stings, food, medications [eg, antibiotics]) characterized by vasodilation, bronchospasm, and increased capillary permeability.  The first-line treatment for anaphylaxis is IM epinephrine, which stimulates adrenergic receptors to reduce vasodilation, prevent further release of inflammatory mediators, and reverse bronchoconstriction.

Clinical manifestations of anaphylaxis include:

Skin reactions (ie, urticaria, flushing) (Option 1)

Respiratory distress (ie, wheezing, dyspnea, stridor) (Option 2)

Swelling of the lips and tongue (ie, angioedema) (Option 3)

Circulatory compromise (ie, hypotension, syncope)

(Option 4)  An S3 heart tone occurs when blood from the atrium is pumped into a noncompliant ventricle and is commonly associated with heart failure, not anaphylaxis.

(Option 5)  Unequal pupils (ie, anisocoria) are associated with conditions such as stroke and head injury, not anaphylaxis.

Educational objective: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction to an allergen characterized by vasodilation, bronchospasm, and increased capillary permeability. Manifestations include urticaria, wheezing, and angioedema. Epinephrine should be administered at the onset of symptoms.

source: Saved NURS401 UWorld capture: 2026-07-07-uworld-practice-quiz-1-answers.md
sourceDetail: UWorld QID 53071
stem: The nurse is assessing a client who has hyperphosphatemia secondary to hypoparathyroidism. Which of the following findings would the nurse expect to obtain?
options: tetany and hyperreflexia
muscle weakness and polyuria
bounding pulse and peripheral edema
abdominal cramping and irregular pulse
rationale:
Hyperphosphatemia (ie, serum phosphate level >4.5 mg/dL [1.45 mmol/L]) is commonly caused by renal injury, tumor lysis syndrome, or hypoparathyroidism.  Increased levels of phosphate reduce the amount of available calcium in the bloodstream.

When serum phosphate levels are elevated, hypocalcemia can occur.  Manifestations of hyperphosphatemia secondary to hypoparathyroidism are consistent with hypocalcemia and include neuromuscular irritability (hyperreflexia), muscle cramping, and tetany (Option 1).

(Option 2)  Muscle weakness and polyuria are manifestations of hypercalcemia.  Elevated serum calcium levels cause a neuromuscular sedating effect and increased urine output to flush out excess calcium.

(Option 3)  Although fluid volume overload caused by kidney disease may lead to hyperphosphatemia, bounding pulse and peripheral edema are not manifestations of hyperphosphatemia.

(Option 4)  Abdominal cramping and irregular pulse are manifestations of hyperkalemia that result from electrical changes within the heart and hyperactivity of the gastrointestinal muscles.

Educational objective: Hyperphosphatemia can lead to secondary hypocalcemia due to increased levels of phosphate binding to calcium within the bloodstream. Manifestations of hypocalcemia include neuromuscular irritability (hyperreflexia), muscle cramping, and tetany.

source: Saved NURS401 UWorld capture: 2026-07-07-uworld-practice-quiz-1-answers.md
sourceDetail: UWorld QID 53020
stem: The nurse has attended a staff education program about fluid and electrolyte imbalances. The nurse should recognize that which of the following factors may contribute to an elevated serum sodium level? Select all that apply.
options: diabetes insipidus
chronic constipation
administration of mannitol
administration of lactated Ringer solution
submersion injury sustained in salt water
correctAnswers: submersion injury sustained in salt water
rationale:
Hypernatremia (serum sodium level >145 mEq/L [145 mmol/L]) leads to increased plasma osmolality.  To correct this imbalance, water is pulled into the extracellular fluid, which leads to cell shrinkage.  This results in neurological clinical manifestations (eg, restlessness, hallucinations) and can lead to permanent damage.

Causes of hypernatremia include:

Diabetes insipidus because the kidneys cannot concentrate urine (Option 1)

Inadequate intake of water

Administration of osmotic diuretics (eg, mannitol), which promote excessive water excretion (Option 3)

Insensible water losses (eg, sweating, hyperventilation)

Increased dietary intake of salt

Submersion injury (eg, near drowning) in salt water, which results in excessive sodium consumption (Option 5)

(Option 2)  Diarrhea causes excess water loss that leads to hypernatremia; constipation does not cause excess water loss.

(Option 4)  Lactated Ringer solution is isotonic and does not increase the risk for hypernatremia.  In contrast, hypertonic saline (eg, 3% sodium chloride) contains an increased concentration of sodium and can contribute to an elevated serum sodium level.

Educational objective: Hypernatremia (serum sodium level >145 mEq/L [145 mmol/L]) leads to neurological clinical manifestations. Causes of hypernatremia include diabetes insipidus, administration of osmotic diuretics, and submersion injuries in salt water.

sourceDetail: UWorld QID 52809
stem: The nurse has attended a staff education program about acid-base imbalances. The nurse should recognize that respiratory acidosis is caused by an elevated
options: serum potassium level
PaCO2
serum ketone level
HCO3−
rationale:
Acid-base imbalances

Imbalance

Common causes

Compensatory mechanism

Metabolic acidosis

Diabetic ketoacidosis

Lactic acidosis

Diarrhea

Drugs (eg, aspirin)

Renal failure (inability to regulate HCO3− & H+ levels)

Hyperventilation blows off CO2, causing ↓ CO2 & ↑ pH

Metabolic alkalosis

Loss of gastric contents (eg, vomiting, nasogastric suction)

Excessive diuresis

Hypoventilation retains CO2, causing ↑ CO2 & ↓ pH

Respiratory acidosis

Hypoventilation (eg, CNS depression, COPD)

Kidneys retain HCO3−, causing ↑ HCO3− & ↑ pH

Respiratory alkalosis

Hyperventilation (eg, anxiety, pain)

Kidneys excrete HCO3−, causing ↓ HCO3− & ↓ pH

COPD = chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Respiratory acidosis is indicated by decreased pH (ie, <7.35) and increased PaCO2 (ie, >45 mm Hg [5.98 kPa]).  HCO3− levels may be normal or increased (ie, compensated).  Respiratory acidosis occurs from hypoventilation secondary to conditions that impair gas exchange within the lungs (eg, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), resulting in elevated PaCO2 (Option 2).

Management of respiratory acidosis focuses on promoting renal function (eg, fluids, electrolyte replacement) and identifying and treating the cause of carbon dioxide gas retention.

(Option 1)  Elevated serum potassium levels (ie, hyperkalemia) can either cause or occur secondary to metabolic acidosis.  The movement of extracellular hydrogen ions into the cell causes intracellular potassium ions to move out of the cell, increasing serum potassium concentration.

(Option 3)  Elevated serum ketone levels are associated with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), a form of metabolic acidosis.  DKA is a life-threatening complication of diabetes mellitus characterized by serum hyperglycemia and intracellular glucose starvation.

(Option 4)  The primary cause of metabolic alkalosis is retention of HCO3− or loss of acid (eg, vomiting, nasogastric suctioning).  Metabolic alkalosis is identified by a pH >7.45, increased HCO3− level, and normal or increased PaCO2.

Educational objective: Respiratory acidosis is indicated by decreased pH (ie, <7.35) and increased PaCO2 (ie, >45 mm Hg [5.98 kPa]) with normal or increased HCO3− levels. Respiratory acidosis occurs from hypoventilation secondary to conditions that impair gas exchange within the lungs (eg, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease).

sourceDetail: UWorld QID 52860
stem: The nurse is assessing a client who has a decreased serum potassium level. Which of the following findings would be consistent with the condition?
options: hypotension
facial twitching
ST segment depression
hyperactive deep tendon reflexes
rationale:
Potassium is an electrolyte that creates an action potential in the membranes of cells that leads to muscle contraction.  Potassium affects cardiac muscle contraction; even slight changes in extracellular concentration can affect cardiac and neuromuscular functions.  Hypokalemia is a decreased serum potassium level that can cause life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias (eg, U wave, prolonged QRS interval, ST segment depression, ventricular tachycardia) because of the impaired repolarization phase (Option 3).

(Option 1)  Hypotension is associated with hypermagnesemia (increased serum magnesium level), not hypokalemia.  Other manifestations of hypermagnesemia include nausea, somnolence, bradycardia, and absent/diminished deep tendon reflexes.

(Option 2)  Chvostek sign (facial twitching after tapping the facial nerve) indicates increased neuromuscular excitability and is associated with hypocalcemia (decreased serum calcium level), not hypokalemia.

(Option 4)  Hypokalemia decreases neuromuscular excitability, which can cause paresthesia (numbness, tingling), muscle cramps, and weakness.  Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes are a manifestation of hypomagnesemia (decreased serum magnesium level), not hypokalemia.

Educational objective: Potassium creates an action potential in the membranes of cells that leads to muscle contraction. Hypokalemia is a decreased serum potassium level that can cause life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias (eg, U wave, ST segment depression, ventricular tachycardia) because of the impaired repolarization phase.

source: Saved NURS401 UWorld capture: 2026-07-07-uworld-practice-quiz-1-answers.md
sourceDetail: UWorld QID 53017
stem: The nurse is caring for a client with hypercalcemia. The nurse should recognize that the client is at risk for developing
options: cardiac dysrhythmias and coma
facial twitching and carpopedal spasms
facial flushing and urinary retention
metabolic alkalosis and bronchial spasms
rationale:
Hypercalcemia (ie, serum calcium level >10.5 mg/dL [2.62 mmol/L]) has a sedating effect that results in decreased neuromuscular excitability, leading to smooth, cardiac, and skeletal muscle dysfunction.  Early stages of hypercalcemia present as polydipsia, constipation, fatigue, and weakness.

Untreated, severe hypercalcemia may escalate to seizures, coma, and cardiac dysrhythmias, such as heart block and ventricular tachycardia.  The nurse should frequently monitor the client's neurological status and provide ECG monitoring for a shortened QT interval and ST segment, as well as a prolonged PR interval (Option 1).

(Option 2)  Chvostek sign (ie, facial twitching induced by tapping the facial nerve) and Trousseau sign (ie, carpopedal spasm induced by occlusion of the brachial artery) indicate increased neuromuscular excitability.  Chvostek and Trousseau signs are associated with hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia.

(Option 3)  Facial flushing and urinary retention are signs of hypermagnesemia; excess serum magnesium inhibits acetylcholine release.

(Option 4)  Metabolic alkalosis and bronchial spasms occur in clients with hypocalcemia.  Metabolic alkalosis occurs due to a lack of ionized calcium in the bloodstream, whereas bronchial spasms occur due to increased neuromuscular irritability.

Educational objective: Hypercalcemia (ie, serum calcium level >10.5 mg/dL [2.62 mmol/L]) has a sedating effect that results in decreased neuromuscular excitability, leading to smooth, cardiac, and skeletal muscle dysfunction. Untreated, severe hypercalcemia may escalate into lethargy, seizures, and cardiac dysrhythmias.

rationale: In ISBAR, Assessment is the nurse’s clinical interpretation of the situation. “I am concerned he may be developing sepsis” states the assessment.
options: "This is Sarah, RN on 4 South calling about Mr. Jones."
"He is postoperative day one following bowel surgery."
"I'd like you to evaluate him and consider blood cultures and IV fluids."
"I am concerned he may be developing sepsis."
correctAnswers: "I am concerned he may be developing sepsis."

stem: A nurse has finished teaching a patient about a newly prescribed anticoagulant. Which statement best uses the teach-back method?
rationale: Teach-back asks the patient to demonstrate or explain the plan in their own words while placing responsibility on the nurse to clarify teaching.
options: "Here's a pamphlet if you forget anything."
"Do you understand everything we discussed?"
"I want to make sure I explained this clearly. Can you show me how you'll take this medication at home?"
"Can you repeat the medication instructions exactly as I explained them?"
correctAnswers: "I want to make sure I explained this clearly. Can you show me how you'll take this medication at home?"

stem: A patient with newly diagnosed heart failure nods throughout discharge teaching and repeatedly says, "I've got it." What should the nurse do next?
rationale: Nodding or saying “I’ve got it” does not verify understanding. Teach-back confirms the patient can apply medication and self-care instructions at home.
options: Accept the patient's statement and complete discharge.
Ask the patient to sign the discharge paperwork.
Use teach-back to verify understanding of medications and self-care.
Ask a family member if they understood the instructions.
correctAnswers: Use teach-back to verify understanding of medications and self-care.

stem: Which patient would not be considered ready for discharge?
rationale: Safe discharge includes access to medications, transportation, follow-up, support, and the ability to carry out the plan. Improved symptoms alone are not enough.
options: A patient with stable vital signs who understands medications and has follow-up appointments scheduled.
A patient whose symptoms have improved but has no transportation to follow-up appointments or access to prescribed medications.
A patient discharged home with home health services after successful teach-back.
A patient transferred to a skilled nursing facility with a complete discharge summary.
correctAnswers: A patient whose symptoms have improved but has no transportation to follow-up appointments or access to prescribed medications.

stem: A nurse reports worsening hypotension and altered mental status to a provider, who dismisses the concerns. The patient's condition continues to deteriorate. What is the nurse's priority action?
rationale: If a serious safety concern is dismissed and the patient is deteriorating, the nurse should escalate through the chain of command rather than waiting or only documenting.
options: Wait one hour and reassess the patient.
Continue documenting vital signs only.
Escalate the concern through the chain of command.
Call the patient's family to discuss concerns.
correctAnswers: Escalate the concern through the chain of command.
